query: management of immature permanent teeth with open apices in pediatric dentistry

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Pulp capping works best in immature teeth with open apices, where the pulp was considered healthy before the trauma. Indications for pulp capping include pulp exposure of a small diameter (less than 1.5 mm) that has only been exposed for a short time, thus minimizing the risk of bacterial invasion. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Favorable outcomes of pulp healing include a normal color of the crown or transient red/grey or yellow discoloration and pulp canal obliteration. There will be no signs of pulp necrosis and infection, and in immature teeth, root development will continue. Additional favorable outcomes include undisturbed development and eruption of the permanent successor teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Partial pulpotomy
retrieved_docs.content: Partial pulpotomy involves amputating the pulp 2 mm below the exposure site, where the tissue is presumed to be healthy. [16] This technique is the treatment of choice for young teeth with open apices when pulp capping is contraindicated, such as when excessive time has elapsed since the trauma or when the exposure diameter is too large. Partial pulpotomy serves as a provisional yet long-term approach, allowing the tooth to remain vital for as long as possible. However, root canal treatment followed by appropriate restoration may eventually be required. Restorative options include freehand or indirect composite resin restoration, fragment reattachment if available, and ceramic restoration.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Treatment / Management -- Follow up Appointment
retrieved_docs.content: Immature teeth where the root has not yet wholly formed have a greater chance of revascularization after soaking in doxycycline. Primary teeth should not undergo replantation as this can damage the underlying permanent tooth germ.

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Pulp capping
retrieved_docs.content: Also known as direct pulp protection, pulp capping involves applying a material with regenerative properties, commonly calcium hydroxide, to small pulp exposures. This technique is indicated when the pulp has been exposed for a short duration, as prolonged exposure increases the risk of bacterial invasion and irreversible inflammation. [14] Clinically, the exposure diameter should not exceed 1.5 mm. The likelihood of success is greater in teeth with healthy pulp before the trauma, immature teeth with open apices, younger teeth, and fractures without concomitant luxation injuries. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Taurodont teeth present challenges across multiple dental specialties. The large pulp chamber and prominent pulp horns increase the risk of pulp exposure due to decay. Variability in pulp chamber size and shape, including apically positioned canal orifices and accessory root canal systems, complicates endodontic therapy. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Crown-Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: The immediate management is to remove the loose fragment, often under local anesthesia, and assess if the crown can be restored. If the tooth is restorable and has no pulpal exposure, cover the exposed dentine with glass ionomer. If the tooth is restorable, but the pulp has been exposed, perform a pulpotomy or root canal treatment depending on the root development stage and fracture level.

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Enamel Fracture
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment options depend on fragment availability and the extent of the lesion. The tooth fragment may be reattached, the edges may be smoothed, and the defect may be restored with resin composite. Clinical and radiographic follow-up is recommended after 2 months and again at 1 year to assess the restoration and look for signs of pulp necrosis, apical periodontitis, and, in immature teeth, impaired root development.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Crown-Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: If the tooth is unrestorable, extract all loose fragments taking care not to damage the permanent successor, leave any firm root fragment in situ, or extract the entire tooth. When the tooth has been retained, a follow-up clinical examination is recommended after one week, six to eight weeks, and one year. A radiographic examination should be done one year following pulpotomy or root canal treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In moderately severe to advanced cases, pulp treatment, extensive restoration, and tooth extractions are required. In these cases, deep sedation or general anesthesia with its inherent complications is needed due to lack of compliance within the age group. Restoration is preferably done with prefabricated stainless steel crowns for primary or permanent teeth with caries. [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Dens invaginatus should be evaluated radiographically with either a series of periapical radiographs from different angles or a CBCT to evaluate the internal structures. Depending on the complexity of the tooth's internal structure or the extent of the invagination, treatment and management strategies of these teeth can range from monitoring to surgical intervention. Treatment has phased into an endodontic approach compared to the previous oral surgery-driven approach with extractions due to the development of better diagnostic aids, such as the CBCT. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the epidemiology of tooth avulsion. Review the treatment regime for an avulsed tooth. Explain the pathophysiology and histopathology of an avulsed tooth. Summarize the complications usually associated with tooth avulsion. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Luxations
retrieved_docs.content: Intrusive luxations in teeth with incomplete root formation may spontaneously re-erupt without intervention. If spontaneous repositioning does not occur within 4 weeks, orthodontic repositioning becomes necessary. Teeth with complete root formation may spontaneously re-erupt if there is less than 3 mm of intrusion; however, surgical repositioning should be initiated if there is no spontaneous eruption within 8 weeks. If there is more than 3 mm of intrusion, the tooth should be repositioned surgically. The tooth should be stabilized in a passive, flexible splint for 4 weeks. While teeth with incomplete root formation are unlikely to develop pulp necrosis, mature teeth almost always experience pulp necrosis, and the health of the pulp should be monitored at subsequent follow-up appointments. Root canal therapy should be considered early. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Luxations
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral luxations require that the tooth be gently relocated back to this alveolar socket under local anesthesia. This can be difficult when the root is “locked” into position by a concomitant alveolar fracture and may require the dentist to disengage the tooth apex before repositioning it. The tooth requires stabilization with a passive and flexible splint for at least 4 weeks, perhaps longer if there are concomitant alveolar fractures. The pulp should be evaluated for necrosis 2 weeks after the injury with sensitivity testing. Teeth with incomplete root formation may experience spontaneous revascularization. However, teeth with complete root formation likely experience pulp necrosis, and root canal therapy is often indicated. The patient is recommended to follow up with a dentist after 2 weeks, 4 weeks, 8 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, teeth with grey discoloration can recover to their original color, become yellowed or remain grey. [18] Teeth with dark discoloration can remain clinically asymptomatic with no radiographic signs of necrosis until natural exfoliation. Root canal treatment is not recommended for discolored teeth unless there are clinical signs of infection of the pulp. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Dens evaginatus/talon cusps have a good prognosis with appropriate management. When less severe, patients may be unaware of their presence, while more severe cases may warrant more invasive intervention. As long as proper restorative or endodontic protocol is followed, teeth that present with dens evaginatus have a good prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.
